content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (analogy / refactor / challenger), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	- archon-lean-lsp: Lean LSP MCP server (project scope) — use for all Lean LSP operations (search, diagnostics, goal inspection)
19	- archon-informal-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning
20	- archon-refactor-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-refactor-agent.py` — invoke the refactor subagent on a directive file
21	- archon-analogy-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-analogy-agent.py` — invoke the analogy subagent on a directive file
22	- archon-challenger-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-challenger-agent.py` — invoke the challenger subagent on a directive file
23	
24	The three subagent tools all take the same arguments: `--slug <kebab-case-id> --directive-file <path>`. Each shells out to `archon subagent <name> ...`, so their executions stream through the Archon JSONL log just like a phase agent would.
25	
26	## Key Files & Permissions
27	
28	All state files are in `.archon/`:
29	
30	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents (analogy / refactor / challenger) | Review Agent | User |
31	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
32	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
33	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
34	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
35	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
36	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
37	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<role>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
38	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
39	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
40	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (refactor may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
41	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | refactor: write (all files; protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); analogy/challenger: read-only on project source | do not edit | write (via comments) |
42	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
43	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | challenger: **write** | do not edit | read |
44	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | analogy: **write** | do not access | read |
45	
46	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent by writing a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/` and passing its path to `archon-refactor-agent.py --directive-file`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
47	
48	## Protected Declarations
49	
50	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
51	
52	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
53	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
54	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
55	
56	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
57	
58	The blueprint uses two active markers.
59	
60	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
61	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
62	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
63	
64	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
65	
66	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
67	
68	## User Interaction
69	
70	Users provide hints in two places:
71	
72	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
73	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
74	
75	## Agent Roles
76	
77	### Plan Agent
78	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
79	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
80	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
81	- Optionally invoke the **subagents** (`analogy`, `refactor`, `challenger`) by shelling out to the corresponding `.claude/tools/archon-<name>-agent.py` script via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for canonical ordering, the directive format for each subagent, and the exact invocation pattern.
82	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
83	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
84	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
85	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
86	
87	### Prover Agent
88	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
89	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
90	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
91	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
92	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
93	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
94	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
95	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
96	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
97	
98	### Subagents (analogy / refactor / challenger)
99	
100	These are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. The plan agent invokes them on demand by running the corresponding `.claude/tools/archon-<name>-agent.py` script via Bash, with `--slug <slug> --directive-file <path>`. Each subagent reads only the files the directive points it at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other plan-agent state — and writes a self-contained report.
101	
102	- **`refactor`** — read `.archon/prompts/refactor.md`. Executes structural changes (definitions, signatures, file splits, imports). Inserts `sorry` at broken proof sites; never fills proofs. Writes report to `.archon/task_results/refactor-<slug>.md`.
103	- **`analogy`** — read `.archon/prompts/analogy.md`. Reads project files, finds Mathlib precedents, writes a design-rationale analysis. Persistent output at `analogies/<slug>.md`; report at `.archon/task_results/analogy-<slug>.md`. Read-only on project source.
104	- **`challenger`** — read `.archon/prompts/challenger.md`. Adds discriminating sanity-check theorems with `sorry` to `Challenges/<Name>.lean`. Provers fill them later. Report at `.archon/task_results/challenger-<slug>.md`. Read-only on target files.
105	
106	The plan agent always reads the full report file after a subagent returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings. None of the subagents spawn other subagents.
107	
108	### Review Agent
109	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
110	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
111	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
112	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
113	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.
114	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md
115	
116	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
117	
118	Two non-agent steps run automatically each iteration to reduce the burden on the agents above:
119	
120	- **Pre-compactors** (`compact-strategy`, `compact-task-pending`, `compact-task-done`, `compact-project-status`): run before plan / review, rewriting oversized state files in place while preserving every actionable detail. Configured under `compaction.*` in `.archon/config.json`. Skipped when files are below threshold.
121	- **`sync_leanok`**: runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status. Replaces what used to be a multi-page review-agent task.
122	
123	Both write inner-git commits (`archon[NNN/precompact/...]`, `archon[NNN/marker-sync]`) so their output is auditable and revertable.

1	# Plan Agent
2	
3	You are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).
4	
5	## Iteration number
6	
7	Your invocation prompt always contains a line `Archon iteration: NNN`. That is the canonical iteration counter — it is what Archon writes to `logs/iter-NNN/` and stamps into commit messages (`archon[NNN/phase]`). The loop also exposes it to subagent tools as the environment variable `ARCHON_ITER_NUM`, so you do not need to pass it on the command line.
8	
9	The session counter under `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` is independent — it counts prover rounds only and is not an iteration number.
10	
11	## Your Job
12	
13	1. Read `USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate user hints into your planning, then clear the file after acting.
14	2. Read `task_results/` — collect prover results from each `<file>.md`, then merge findings into `task_pending.md` (update attempts) and `task_done.md` (migrate resolved theorems). Clear processed result files.
15	3. Read `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md` to recover context — do not repeat documented dead ends.
16	4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` — if review journal sessions exist, read the latest session's `summary.md` and `recommendations.md` for the review agent's analysis. Also read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists — it contains cumulative progress, known blockers, and reusable proof patterns. Use these findings when setting objectives.
17	5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** — see "Long-arc Strategy" below. Always do this *before* writing prover objectives or invoking subagents: it forces you to think about how this iteration fits into the path to project completion, instead of optimizing locally.
18	6. Evaluate each task: is it completed, can it be completed, and if not why not? Should a subagent be invoked?
19	7. Verify prover reports independently (check sorry count and compilation) — do not trust self-reports.
20	8. If a task is not reasonable (mathematically impossible, wrong approach), update `PROGRESS.md` with a corrected plan.
21	9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see "Blueprint-based informal content" below). Ensure the blueprint files are always consistent with the current state of the project.
22	10. Optionally invoke subagents (see "Subagent delegation" below).
23	11. Set clear, self-contained objectives for the next prover iteration.
24	12. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to write or edit formal proofs, stop immediately and return to your supervisory role.
25	13. Detect critical issues in the project (such as wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms, etc.) and address them, even if they have been present since the beginning.
26	
27	**Write permissions**: You may write to `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `USER_HINTS.md` (to clear it), `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (to write/update informal proof), and `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` (to add macro definitions for any non-standard LaTeX commands you use). You must NOT edit `.lean` files or `task_results/` files.
28	
29	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke the refactor subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/refactor-<slug>-directive.md` and pass its path to `archon-refactor-agent.py --directive-file`. See "Subagent delegation" below for the full pattern. If you see `REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` mentioned in older `STRATEGY.md` / `task_pending.md` / `PROGRESS.md` content, treat it as historical noise and prune it out as you rewrite.
30	
31	**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on — nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced in that section's headings/bullets. If you mention an off-limits file (e.g. "### 3. The protected files (`Genus.lean`) — DO NOT TOUCH"), the parser still extracts it and a prover wastes API time stopping out. Two rules:
32	
33	- Do not list off-limits / DO-NOT-TOUCH / "skip this" files in `## Current Objectives`. Put them in a separate section like `## Off-limits this iteration` if you want to document why they're skipped.
34	- The objectives section should contain exactly the files you want a prover to attack this round, one per heading or bullet.
35	
36	**Important**: You should **NEVER** propose adding new axioms. If axioms are already present, you should remove them.
37	
38	## Boundary: Mathematical Intent, Not Lean Syntax
39	
40	**Your output is mathematical intent. The prover's output is Lean syntax. Never cross this boundary.**
41	
42	Concretely:
43	
44	- **You MAY use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle`** to check whether a piece of Mathlib infrastructure *exists* — answering questions like "does Mathlib have a localization theorem for basic opens?" This informs your informal proof sketch and tells you whether to flag a potential gap. Keep in mind that the Prover will also use these tools. 
45	- **You MUST NOT use `lean_run_code`** to validate candidate proof bodies, search for working tactic sequences, or confirm that a specific Lean expression type-checks. If you find yourself writing or testing Lean tactic code, stop — that is the prover's job.
46	
47	## Protected declarations
48	
49	Read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. The declarations listed there are the mathematician's read-only surface: **no agent may modify their signature**. As plan agent:
50	
51	- Do not assign an objective that would require changing a protected signature.
52	- Moving a protected declaration to a different file is allowed (the refactor subagent will update the YAML path), but renaming or re-signing is not.
53	
54	## References
55	
56	A paragraph-by-paragraph summary of every informal source is pasted into your prompt from `references/summary.md`. Read it every iteration. Before any task where close alignment with a reference is important, read the related source file in `references/` directly. Do not rely on memory or summaries alone.
57	
58	If relevant, you can use Web Search to find new references, in which case you should update `references/summary.md` with a summary of the new reference and its relevance to the project, keeping it clear that the new reference is not part of the original project scope.
59	
60	## Blueprint-based informal content
61	
62	This project uses a blueprint (plasTeX + `leanblueprint`). Informal proof live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file. The slug mapping is:
63	
64	```
65	Lean file  Algebra/WLocal.lean  →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex
66	Lean file  Core.lean            →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex
67	```
68	
69	`blueprint/src/content.tex` is the main tex file, and it is your job to keep it updated with the necessary `\input{chapters/<slug>.tex}`. Your job is also to ensure that the blueprint is correct and aligned with the intended content of the project, which includes aligning the blueprint with the Lean files as they evolve. The blueprints should not be sketchy but should instead contain rigorous proofs, at the same level as a textbook or paper.
70	
71	**Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the informal content the prover needs.** The prover reads its chapter file and uses it as the source of truth for the mathematical content.
72	
73	- You can create/modify/rename/delete blueprint chapters as needed, **as long as** you keep `blueprint/src/content.tex` updated and add the corresponding objective in `PROGRESS.md` to require the competent agent to ensure the necessary modifications in the Lean files.
74	- The blueprints are considered by the other agents as the source of truth for informal content, so they should always be consistent with the current state of the project; any mistake or inconsistency should be fixed as soon as possible.
75	
76	### What to write in a chapter file
77	
78	For each declaration the prover will need to handle, the chapter should contain a block like this:
79	
80	```latex
81	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
82	  \label{thm:some_label}
83	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
84	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
85	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
86	\end{theorem}
87	
88	\begin{proof}
89	  \uses{thm:another_result}
90	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
91	  so the dependency graph stays accurate. Use as much detail as the prover would
92	  need to formalize — a one-liner is rarely enough.
93	\end{proof}
94	```
95	
96	**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** Write the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover translates your mathematics into Lean; you do not pre-translate it for them.
97	
98	**Macros the prover relies on:**
99	
100	- `\lean{foo.bar}` — declares which Lean name this block corresponds to
101	- `\leanok` — added by the prover once formalization is complete (you do not add it)
102	- `\mathlibok` — added when the declaration already exists in Mathlib. Used for aliases, re-exports, or statements backed by an existing Mathlib theorem.
103	
104	### LaTeX macros — define before you use
105	
106	If you write a non-standard command in a chapter, define it in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using it.
107	
108	### Record where the informal content lives
109	
110	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective, record which blueprint chapter backs it. Example:
111	
112	```markdown
113	1. **`Algebra/WLocal.lean`** — resolve 3 sorries. Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex` (theorems `thm:wLocal_iff`, `thm:wLocal_of_surjective`).
114	```
115	
116	The prover will read the chapter file mentioned here.
117	
118	## Long-arc Strategy
119	
120	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from its current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it — the prover, refactor, and review agents never read or write it. The mathematician reads it, so keep it human-readable.
121	
122	Read it early in every iteration, before deciding which sorries to assign or whether to invoke a subagent, so the next iteration is grounded in the bigger picture.
123	
124	Update it after processing prover/review results and before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint. Describe the *current* plan only: the remaining steps from today's state to the end-state, in roughly the order they need to happen, with a rough effort estimate (iterations, LOC) per step. Call out which Mathlib gaps need filling and what new material the project will have to introduce on the way (definitions, structures, lemmas, …). Do not narrate past iterations — the Revision log captures history.
125	
126	Aim for the big picture, not the details. Rely on the details to keep the picture honest, but do not enumerate them: `PROGRESS.md` and the blueprint hold the specifics. The mathematician should be able to read the strategy.
127	
128	If nothing strategically changed this iteration, leave the body alone and add nothing to the Revision log. When the strategy changes, rewrite the affected parts in place and append one bullet to the Revision log explaining *why*: `- iter NNN — <one-line reason>`.
129	
130	Indicate clearly in the beginning the current estimation of iterations and LOC remaining (e.g., in a tabular).
131	
132	Because the file is cumulative, if it becomes too long, for readability and context management, you can remove, reduce or merge old iteration notes in the Revision log; you can also remove old strategy steps that have been completed.
133	
134	## Feasibility Gate
135	
136	When facing difficult tasks, you and your agents should always try to think harder and should never delegate the task to other iterations or other agents. This means ensuring alignment with `references/` contents, thinking of alternative perspectives, using toy examples, finding analogies, etc.
137	
138	You should always question your previous work. The project (blueprints, Lean files, or even references sometimes) might contain wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms included for convenience, etc. If you identify such a critical issue, whether new or present since many iterations, you should absolutely address it, for instance by invoking the refactor subagent.
139	
140	You should also be resilient when encountering obstacles and consider whether `Mathlib` contains the necessary infrastructure to solve the problem, or whether the current strategy requires filling its gaps. You can use `lean_leansearch` or `lean_loogle` to check if the required lemmas, type classes, or API functions exist in Mathlib — but limit this to existence checks, not proof exploration. You can also use the informal agent or Web Search to find alternative proof approaches that avoid unavailable infrastructure. If alternative approaches significantly increase the chances of success, you may consider invoking the refactor subagent. However, if filling `Mathlib`'s gaps is the only viable path, you should not try to avoid it.
141	
142	## Subagent delegation
143	
144	You have access to three subagents that you may invoke during your run, **before** writing prover objectives. They are optional — invoke each one only when it is justified for the iteration. Each call costs API time and inflates your context — do not invoke reflexively. State explicitly in your reasoning why each call is needed.
145	
146	### How to invoke a subagent
147	
148	Each subagent is a Python wrapper script in `.claude/tools/`. You invoke it via the **Bash tool** — not the Agent tool. The pattern is the same for all three subagents:
149	
150	1. Choose a kebab-case **slug** (e.g. `split-wlocal`, `quotient-vs-coequalizer`, `wlocal-correctness`). Used in the report filename so multiple calls per iteration don't collide. Each call within an iteration must use a distinct slug.
151	2. Write the directive to a tempfile at `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-directive.md` (NNN is the canonical iteration number from your invocation prompt). Use `Write` to create the file.
152	3. Run the wrapper via Bash:
153	
154	```
155	python3 .claude/tools/archon-<role>-agent.py \
156	  --slug <slug> \
157	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-directive.md
158	```
159	
160	4. The wrapper prints a one-line summary to stdout and exits 0 on success, non-zero on failure.
161	
162	The directive must be **fully self-contained**. The subagent does not read `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other plan-agent state. It reads only what you tell it to read plus the blueprint chapters for the affected files. Indicate in the directive which files the subagent should read.
163	
164	The iteration number does not need to be passed as a CLI argument — the loop sets `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` in the environment, and the wrapper reads it from there.
165	
166	### The three subagents
167	
168	- **`analogy`** — finds existing Mathlib code along with the design choices behind it, so you can see what Mathlib authors did in situations analogous to yours. Use when you are uncertain which of several routes to take and you believe a similar situation has arisen in Mathlib. Output is persistent under `analogies/<slug>.md` and may be re-read by future iterations. Read-only on project source. Directive format: see "Analogy directive" below.
169	
170	- **`refactor`** — executes structural changes (definitions, signatures, file splits, imports). Use when proof-filling alone cannot fix the problem. Inserts `sorry` at broken proof sites; never fills proofs. See "Refactor subagent" below for details.
171	
172	- **`challenger`** — creates a new file `Challenges/<Name>.lean` with discriminating sanity-check theorems (with `sorry`) that envelope a definition's intended behavior. The objectives it adds are not directly useful for the global project, but solving them gives confidence that the intermediate definitions introduced for the global project are correct and easy to use. Use to lock in what a new or doubted definition must satisfy. Provers will fill the sorries later, which confirms the definition is usable in practice. Directive format: see "Challenger directive" below.

174	### Canonical ordering
175	
176	When multiple subagents are needed in one iteration, the canonical (optional) order is:
177	
178	1. **`analogy`** — gather precedent first, before deciding what to change.
179	2. **`refactor`** — make structural changes, informed by analogy findings.
180	3. **`challenger`** — envelope the resulting definitions or definitions already existing in the project.
181	4. **Write prover objectives** — only after the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
182	
183	You may invoke each subagent multiple times in one iteration if justified (e.g. two unrelated refactors, or analogies on two distinct questions). Each call needs a distinct slug.
184	
185	### After each subagent returns
186	
187	The subagent writes its full report to `.archon/task_results/<role>-<slug>.md`. The wrapper's stdout is a one-line summary. You must:
188	
189	1. **Read the full report file.** The stdout summary is intentionally compressed.
190	2. **Verify the work independently.** For refactor: check sorry count and compilation using `lean_diagnostic_messages` and `sorry_analyzer` only. For challenger: check that `Challenges/<Name>.lean` compiles. For analogy: spot-check that the cited Mathlib paths exist using `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle`.
191	3. **Archive the report** to `logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it. Use `cp` to copy, not move — the `task_results/` file stays so future iterations can find it.
192	4. **Update `STRATEGY.md`** if the subagent's findings changed the long-arc plan (e.g. analogy revealed Mathlib has the structure already; refactor split a file you'd been treating as monolithic).
193	5. **Update `PROGRESS.md`** with whatever new prover objectives the subagent's output enables.

290	## Providing Informal Content to the Prover
291	
292	The prover performs significantly better when given rich informal mathematical guidance. Before assigning a task, you must ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof or proof sketch.
293	
294	**How to provide informal content:**
295	
296	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): Write directly in `PROGRESS.md` under the task objectives. Example: "Key idea: use Bolzano-Weierstrass to extract a convergent subsequence, then show the limit satisfies the property."
297	
298	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): Write as comments in the corresponding `.lean` file, above the declaration with `sorry`. Use `/- ... -/` block comments.
299	
300	- **Long content** (a full proof sketch, paper summary, or multi-step construction): Write in the relevant chapter `.tex` file in the blueprint. Reference the blueprint chapter in `PROGRESS.md` next to the objective.
301	
302	- **Other possibilities**: The above methods should be prioritized, but if relevant, you may use Web Search to find new references, or write a separate markdown file in the project (e.g. `informal_sketches/some_lemma.md`) and link to it from `PROGRESS.md`.
303	
304	**No matter which method you choose, always record in `PROGRESS.md`** where the informal content is located, so the prover can obtain it without searching.
305	
306	**All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic.** Describe the proof in terms of mathematical objects, maps, and properties. Do not write Lean tactic sequences, term-mode proof expressions, or rewrite chains — those are the prover's output, not yours. If you find yourself writing `rw [← foo] ▸ bar.baz _`, stop and rephrase mathematically.
307	
308	**When the reference is vague** (e.g., "by Hiblot 1975" without proof details):
309	
310	1. Use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate an informal proof sketch from an external model.
311	2. Use Web Search to find the referenced paper and extract the key proof steps.
312	3. Write the result into a file and record the path in `PROGRESS.md`.
313	4. Do this **before** assigning the task to the prover — don't send the prover in blind.
314	
315	## Recognizing Prover Failure Modes
316	
317	### "Mathlib doesn't have it" — Missing Infrastructure
318	
319	The #1 failure mode. The prover reports that a sorry is unfillable because Mathlib lacks the infrastructure, then stops.
320	
321	**Your response:** This is YOUR job to solve, not the prover's. Never just pass it back with "try harder." You must actively find an alternative proof route:
322	
323	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`) — ask it: "Prove X without using [the missing infrastructure]. Only use tools available in Lean 4 Mathlib." Get a concrete alternative proof sketch.
324	2. **Use Web Search** — find the referenced paper or alternative proofs of the same result that avoid the missing infrastructure.
325	3. **Decompose differently** — break the problem into sub-lemmas where each sub-lemma only needs available infrastructure. The prover can implement Mathlib-level lemmas if you give it clear, self-contained goals.
326	4. **Check `mathlib-unavailable-theorems.md`** — if the missing infrastructure is in a known-unavailable domain, don't waste time looking for it. Focus on detours.
327	5. **If the infrastructure gap is in the definition itself** — invoke the refactor subagent to change the definition so it doesn't require the missing infrastructure downstream.
328	
329	Write the re-routed informal proof into the corresponding chapter `.tex` file (as a `\begin{proof} ... \end{proof}` body), then reassign the task to the prover. Do not reassign without providing an alternative in the chapter. The re-routed proof must be written mathematically — not as Lean syntax.
330	
331	### Wrong Construction — Building on a Flawed Foundation
332	
333	The prover chose a wrong construction and the sorry is mathematically unfillable, but the prover keeps trying instead of backtracking.
334	
335	**Your response:** If the fix is within a single file, instruct the prover to revert. If the fix requires cross-file changes, invoke the refactor subagent. Update the chapter `.tex` with the correct construction before the next prover round.
336	
337	### Not Using Web Search
338	
339	The prover searches only within Mathlib and gives up when it finds nothing, even when the blueprint references a specific paper.
340	
341	**Your response:** Explicitly instruct: "Use Web Search to find [paper name/arXiv ID], read the proof, decompose it into sub-lemmas, and formalize step by step." Update the chapter with the retrieved proof sketch.
342	
343	### Early Stopping on Hard Problems
344	
345	The prover stops and reports "done" when the remaining sorry requires significant effort.
346	
347	**Your response:** Reject the report. Break the hard problem into smaller sub-goals in the chapter `.tex` and assign them one at a time. Frame it as: "Formalize just sub-lemma L1 from the blueprint, then report back."
348	
349	### Using tricks (e.g. axioms, ad-hoc definitions, weakening hypotheses) to bypass hard parts
350	
351	The prover introduces new axioms or definitions that aren't in the blueprint to fill sorries, then reports completion. You should also never propose such tricks as a plan agent.
352	
353	**Your response:** Reject the report. Such tricks should not be accepted; they should be documented and then removed. You should then try to understand why this route was chosen and ensure that it will not be reproduced.
354	
355	## Assessing Prover Progress
356	
357	### Three Indicators
358	
359	| Indicator | Meaning |
360	|-----------|---------|
361	| Sorry count (decreasing) | Direct progress — a sorry was filled |
362	| Code line count (increasing) | Infrastructure building — helpers, definitions |
363	| `\leanok` marks added | Prover confirmed formalization against the blueprint |
364	
365	Line count increasing + sorry count unchanged = the prover is building infrastructure. This is real progress.
366	
367	Line count unchanged + sorry count unchanged = zero progress.
368	
369	### Deep Stuck vs Early Abandonment
370	
371	| Pattern | Diagnosis | Response |
372	|---------|-----------|----------|
373	| 800+ lines, 2-3 sorries left | Deep stuck — needs math hint or infrastructure | Provide informal guidance via informal agent, suggest specific decomposition |
374	| <200 lines, sorry remaining | Early abandonment — prover gave up too quickly | Push harder: break into sub-goals, provide richer informal content |
375	
376	## Verification
377	
378	After a prover reports completion, always verify independently using only these two tools:
379	
380	1. Check sorry count: `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/sorry_analyzer.py" <file> --format=summary`
381	2. Check compilation: `lean_diagnostic_messages(file)`
382	
383	Do not use `lean_run_code`, `lean_verify`, or any other Lean execution tool during verification — those are the prover's instruments. If `lean_diagnostic_messages` returns errors, report the error to the prover with context; do not attempt to diagnose or fix the Lean code yourself.
384	
385	3. Check axioms: confirm no new `axiom` declarations appear in the diff
386	4. Check blueprint consistency: `leanblueprint checkdecls` flags Lean names in the blueprint that don't exist. Run this after the prover has renamed or removed declarations.
387	
388	Never advance to the next stage based solely on the prover's word.
389	
390	## Dependency graph
391	
392	Before scoping objectives, run the bundled dependency-graph script instead of reconstructing the dependency map by hand. It parses every `.lean` file's imports plus every `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` for `\lean{…}` / `\uses{…}` / `\proves{…}` / `\leanok` / `\notready`, and emits a JSON view of the whole project in well under a second:
393	
394	```
395	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=json
396	```
397	
398	Use `--format=summary` for a one-screen overview, `--format=dot` if you need to share the graph elsewhere. Read this once per iteration to decide objective ordering — files with no upstream sorries should be formalised first, downstream files later.
399	
400	## Decomposition Strategy
401	
402	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem:
403	
404	1. Read the blueprint chapter to identify sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, L3, ...).
405	2. Read the files in `references/` related to it, if any, to ensure you understand and align with the original proof.
406	3. Check if the chapter is detailed enough — if not, expand it first (using informal agent / Web Search).
407	4. Assign one sub-lemma at a time: "Fill sorry for L1 only".
408	5. After L1 is done, verify, then assign L2.
409	6. Record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
410	
411	## Context Management
412	
413	Each prover iteration starts with fresh context. The prover does not remember previous iterations.
414	
415	- Provide **self-contained** objectives in `PROGRESS.md` — include all context the prover needs.
416	- Point the prover at its blueprint chapter — that is where the mathematical content lives.
417	- When a prover gets stuck on the same failure across multiple iterations, it is re-discovering the same dead end. Change the approach entirely — do not just repeat "try again".
418	- Document dead ends in `PROGRESS.md` so the prover doesn't repeat them.
419	
420	## Multi-Agent Coordination
421	
422	Provers run in parallel — one agent per file. Your objectives must be structured accordingly.
423	
424	### Writing objectives
425	
426	Number each objective clearly (1, 2, 3, ...). Each objective maps to **exactly one file**. Never assign two objectives to the same file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside.
427	
428	```markdown
429	## Current Objectives
430	
431	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorry in `filter_convergence` (line 156). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex` (see `thm:filter_convergence`).
432	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Measure.tex`. Use MeasureTheory.Measure.restrict_apply with finite spanning sets.
433	3. **`Topology.lean`** — Fill sorry in `compact_embedding` (line 203). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Topology.tex`. Straightforward from CompactSpace + isClosedEmbedding.
434	```
435	
436	### Balancing difficulty
437	
438	Estimate the relative difficulty of each objective. If one file has significantly harder sorries than others, consider decomposing it into helper lemmas first (in a prior plan iteration) so the prover agent has smaller, more tractable goals. The goal is for all agents to finish around the same time.
439	
440	While you should balance difficulty, you should also aim at making concrete progress — therefore avoid giving shallow or trivial objectives.
441	
442	### Agent count
443	
444	- **Agent count = file count**: if 24 files need work, write 24 objectives — one per file. Do not artificially batch or limit the number of objectives. The shell script handles parallelism.
445	- If an experiment is restarted, check the compilation status of every target `.lean` file before planning. Prioritize files that still have `sorry` or compilation errors. Do not redo completed work.
446	
447	## Stage Transitions
448	
449	When all objectives in the current stage are met, advance `PROGRESS.md` to the next stage:
450	
451	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (when all statements are formalized)
452	- `prover` → `polish` (when all sorries are filled and verified)
453	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (when proofs are clean and compile)

28	### 1. Rebuilt `h_mod_X_i` to be a **literal** `e_i.toAddEquiv.module R`
29	
30	`letI` (not `have`) so the module instance registers with typeclass synthesis,
31	and `exact e_i.toAddEquiv.module R` (not `convert h`) so the underlying smul
32	is exactly `e_i.toEquiv.smul R` — i.e. `r • x = e_i.symm (r • e_i x)` by `rfl`
33	(`Equiv.smul_def` is itself `rfl`).
34	
35	```lean
36	letI h_mod_X₁ : Module R scK₀.X₁ := by
37	  dsimp only [scK₀, K₀, cechCochain, cechComplexFunctor,
38	    toModuleKSheaf, toModuleKPresheaf_obj]
39	  letI := h_mod_pi₁
40	  exact e₁.toAddEquiv.module R
41	-- (analogous for X₂ and X₃; X₃ pre-rewrites `(up ℕ).next n` via `CochainComplex.next`)
42	```
43	
44	The iter-076 analysis claim that `convert h` introduces "residual eta
45	congruence" turned out to be **partially correct in spirit but mis-diagnosed
46	in detail**: the actual blocker was the use of `have` (which does NOT register
47	as a typeclass instance), not the use of `convert`. With `letI` + `exact`,
48	the instance is both literal AND registered. The `Module R scK₀.X_i` instance
49	that typeclass synthesis finds for `r • x` is now literally
50	`e_i.toAddEquiv.module R` (visible in the goal state as
51	`h_mod_X₁ : Module ↑R ↑scK₀.X₁ := id (AddEquiv.module (↑R) e₁.toAddEquiv)`).

53	### 2. Closed `f_R.map_smul'`
54	
55	Goal: `(scK₀.f)(r • x) = (RingHom.id R) r • (scK₀.f x)`.
56	
57	```lean
58	intro r x
59	change (ConcreteCategory.hom scK₀.f) (e₁.symm (r • e₁ x)) =
60	  e₂.symm (r • e₂ ((ConcreteCategory.hom scK₀.f) x))
61	apply e₂.injective
62	rw [LinearEquiv.apply_symm_apply, h_diff_pi_smul_f r (e₁ x),
63	  LinearEquiv.symm_apply_apply]
64	```
65	
66	The `change` step relies on the literal `h_mod_X₁`/`h_mod_X₂` instances:
67	- `r • x = e₁.symm (r • e₁ x)` by `rfl` (literal `h_mod_X₁`).
68	- `r • (scK₀.f x) = e₂.symm (r • e₂ (scK₀.f x))` by `rfl` (literal `h_mod_X₂`).
69	- `(RingHom.id R) r = r` by `rfl`.
70	
71	After the `change`, applying `e₂.injective` and rewriting via
72	`LinearEquiv.apply_symm_apply` (collapsing the outer `e₂ ∘ e₂.symm`),
73	`h_diff_pi_smul_f r (e₁ x)` (which fires because the resulting goal
74	matches its statement at `y := e₁ x`), and `LinearEquiv.symm_apply_apply`
75	(collapsing `e₁.symm (e₁ x)` to `x`) closes the goal.

88

6	import Mathlib

8	/-!
9	# The symmetric monoidal structure on `X.Modules`
10	
11	Phase C step C0 (per `STRATEGY.md`): the category of $\mathcal O_X$-modules
12	on a scheme $X$ carries a canonical symmetric monoidal structure, with
13	tensor product given by sheafification of the presheaf tensor product and
14	tensor unit given by the structure sheaf $\mathcal O_X$ itself.
15	
16	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Modules_Monoidal.tex`.
17	
18	## Status (iteration 077 — refactor-subagent scaffold)
19	
20	This file scaffolds the three signatures (`tensorObj`,
21	`instMonoidalCategoryStruct`, `instMonoidalCategory`) needed by Phase C
22	step C1 (refining `LineBundle` from `CommRing.Pic Γ(X, ⊤)` to the
23	invertible-object definition). The bodies are `sorry` for the prover
24	rounds iter-078+ to fill, using:
25	
26	- `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory` (presheaf-side structure)
27	- `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` (pointwise tensor)
28	- `PresheafOfModules.sheafification` (left adjoint to inclusion)
29	- `SheafOfModules.toSheafCompSheafToPresheafIso` (the comparison data)
30	
31	## Mathematical content
32	
33	For two $\mathcal O_X$-modules $M$ and $N$,
34	$$
35	  M \otimes_{\mathcal O_X} N
36	    := \mathrm{sheafify}(M_{\mathrm{psh}} \otimes_{\mathcal O_X, \mathrm{psh}} N_{\mathrm{psh}}).
37	$$
38	The associator/unitors/braiding on the sheaf side are sheafifications of their
39	presheaf counterparts; the coherence axioms transfer via the universal
40	property of sheafification.
41	-/
42	
43	set_option autoImplicit false
44	
45	universe u
46	
47	open CategoryTheory MonoidalCategory
48	
49	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules
50	
51	variable (X : Scheme.{u})
52	
53	/-- The tensor product of two $\mathcal O_X$-modules: sheafify the presheaf
54	tensor product of the underlying presheaves-of-modules. -/
55	noncomputable def tensorObj (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules := sorry
56	
57	/-- The symmetric monoidal category structure on `X.Modules`: the tensor
58	product is `tensorObj`, the tensor unit is the structure sheaf $\mathcal O_X$,
59	and the associator, unitors, and braiding are inherited from the presheaf
60	side through sheafification. -/
61	noncomputable instance instMonoidalCategoryStruct :
62	    MonoidalCategoryStruct (X.Modules) := sorry
63	
64	/-- The pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms transfer from the presheaf side
65	through the universal property of sheafification. -/
66	noncomputable instance instMonoidalCategory :
67	    MonoidalCategory (X.Modules) := sorry
68	
69	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules
70

**Mathematical content.** The `d_app` field must show `D_X.d ((f.c.app U).hom (φ_g'.app U a)) = 0`, where `D_X = derivation' φ_fg'` is the universal derivation pushed forward along `f`, and `φ_g'`, `φ_fg'` are the canonical morphisms encoding the universal derivations on $\Omega_{Y/S}$ and $\Omega_{X/S}$ respectively.

The mathematical fact: every element in the image of `f.c.app U ∘ φ_g'.app U` is in the kernel of `D_X.d` because it factors through the universal-derivation structure on $\Omega_{X/S}$. Concretely, `(f.c.app U).hom (φ_g'.app U a)` is a section that factors through `(φ_fg'.app (f⁻¹U)).hom` of some lift `(ζ.app U) a`, and the universal-derivation axiom says `Derivation'.d` annihilates anything pulled back through `φ_fg'`.

The technical blocker: the identity
$$
  \varphi_{g'} \;\circ\; f.c \;=\; \mathrm{adj}_f.\mathrm{unit} \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforward}\,f).\mathrm{map}\,\varphi_{fg'}
$$
is no longer `rfl` against current Mathlib: it asserts the pullback composition equality `pullback f ∘ pullback g = pullback (f≫g)`, which holds up to iso (by uniqueness of left adjoints — both sides are left adjoint to `pushforward (f≫g) = pushforward g ∘ pushforward f`) but is not definitional, since `pullback := (Opens.map _).op.lan` is built via the left Kan extension's universal property.

   ```
   ζ : pullback g S.presheaf ⟶ (pushforward f).obj (pullback (f≫g) S.presheaf)
   ```
   via `adj_fg.homEquiv.symm` of a specific morphism `pullback g S.presheaf ⟶ pullback (f≫g) S.presheaf` (dual to the η in beta). Then pointwise:
   ```
   (f.c.app U).hom (φ_g'.app U a) = (φ_fg'.app (f⁻¹U)).hom ((ζ.app U) a)
   ```
   This puts the LHS in the image of `φ_fg'.app`, and `Derivation'.d_app _ _` closes the goal.

2. **Pullback-composition iso** (alternative). Search Mathlib for a natural iso `pullback f ∘ pullback g ≅ pullback (f≫g)` of `TopCat.Presheaf` functors. Candidate names to try via `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle`: `lan_comp`, `lanCompIso`, `Functor.compLeftAdjoint`, `Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq`. If found, use it to rewrite `(f.c.app U).hom (φ_g'.app U a)` into the form `(φ_fg'.app …).hom (...)`.

**Mathematical content.** The statement `h_diff_pi_smul_f` packages: for `r : R` and `y : ∀ i, Z₁ i` (where `Z₁ i = X.basicOpenCover_inter f i`, the affine basic open at index `i`), the f-side cochain differential `scK₀.f` applied to the smul `r • y` equals `r • (scK₀.f y)`, both computed via the canonical `e_i : ↑(∏ᶜ Z_i) ≃ scK₀.X_i` linear equivalences.

The proof requires unfolding the 5-layer functor stack and chasing the R-linearity through each layer. The decomposition (documented in-place at L1009–L1067) is:

- **S1: `funext j`** to get the goal componentwise in the codomain index `j`. (NOT in indexed-sum form — this was the iter-075 mistake; the goal post-`funext` is `(scK₀.f) (r • y) j = r • (scK₀.f y) j`.)

- **S5: `Finset.smul_sum`** distributes `r •` across the alternating sum.
- **S6: `Finset.sum_congr rfl fun k _ => ?_`** reduces to per-summand R-linearity.
- **S7:** Per-summand `r • (restriction along δ_k applied to y_{j∘δ_k}) = restriction along δ_k applied to (r • y_{j∘δ_k})`. Decompose via: (i) `Pi.smul_apply` on LHS at output index `j`, (ii) `RingHom.map_mul` distributes the restriction across `r * y_{j∘δ_k}`, (iii) `algebraMap_naturality` says the restriction respects the algebraMap (the R-action factors through the structure-sheaf restriction map), (iv) reverse `Pi.smul_apply` for `h_mod_pi₂` at output index `j`.

### 3. `AlgebraicJacobian/Modules/Monoidal.lean` — fill the three Phase-C-C0 bodies

**File status.** 3 syntactic sorries; file compiles cleanly (0 errors). Scaffolded by the iter-077 refactor subagent. Sorries: L55 `tensorObj`, L62 `instMonoidalCategoryStruct`, L67 `instMonoidalCategory`.

**Goal.** Fill all three sorries so that the file declares a complete `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance for every scheme `X`. This is the Phase C step C0 closure — unblocks the iter-079+ refinement of `LineBundle` to invertible $\mathcal O_X$-modules.

**Mathematical content** (see blueprint `Modules_Monoidal.tex`, theorem `thm:Modules_MonoidalCategory`):

For two $\mathcal O_X$-modules $M, N$, the tensor product is
$$
  M \otimes_{\mathcal O_X} N \;:=\; \mathrm{sheafify}\bigl(M_{\mathrm{psh}} \otimes_{\mathcal O_X, \mathrm{psh}} N_{\mathrm{psh}}\bigr),
$$
where $(-)_{\mathrm{psh}}$ is the forgetful functor from $\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,\mathcal O_X$ to $\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal O_X$, and the presheaf tensor product is the pointwise one supplied by Mathlib's `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`. The tensor unit is the structure sheaf $\mathcal O_X$ regarded as the trivial $\mathcal O_X$-module. The associator, unitors, and braiding are sheafifications of their presheaf counterparts; the pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherences transfer via the universal property of sheafification (sheafification is a left adjoint to inclusion, so commutes with colimits, and tensor is a colimit-preserving bifunctor).

**Recipe.**

**(a) `tensorObj` (L55).** The body should construct `X.Modules` from the sheafification of the presheaf tensor product. The shortest path uses three Mathlib pieces:
- `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` — the pointwise presheaf tensor product.
- `PresheafOfModules.sheafification` — the sheafification functor (left adjoint to the forgetful `SheafOfModules → PresheafOfModules`).

```
noncomputable def tensorObj (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification _).obj
    (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj M.val N.val)
```

noncomputable instance instMonoidalCategoryStruct :
    MonoidalCategoryStruct (X.Modules) where
  tensorObj := tensorObj X
  tensorUnit := …  -- the structure sheaf as a SheafOfModules
  whiskerLeft := …
  whiskerRight := …
  tensorHom := …
  associator := …
  leftUnitor := …
  rightUnitor := …
```
The whisker/tensorHom/associator/unitors all transfer from the presheaf side by sheafifying the presheaf-side morphism. The prover may find it easier to provide one comprehensive `where`-block than to delegate via `inferInstance` chains.

**(c) `instMonoidalCategory` (L67).** Provides the pentagon, triangle, and hexagon coherence axioms. Each transfers from the presheaf side via the universal property of sheafification; if `Sheaf.monoidalCategory` (Mathlib's generic instance) is applicable, defer to it; otherwise build the four `*.naturality`/coherence proofs explicitly using `SheafOfModules.hom_ext` to descend to the presheaf-side check.

\begin{definition}[Tensor product of $\mathcal O_X$-modules]
  \label{def:Modules_tensorObj}
  Let $X$ be a scheme and $M, N \in X.\mathrm{Modules}$. The \emph{tensor product} $M \otimes_{\mathcal O_X} N$ is defined as the sheafification of the presheaf tensor product:
  \[
    M \otimes_{\mathcal O_X} N \;:=\; \mathrm{sheafify}\bigl(M_{\mathrm{psh}} \otimes_{\mathcal O_X, \mathrm{psh}} N_{\mathrm{psh}}\bigr),
  \]
  where $(-)_{\mathrm{psh}}$ is the forgetful functor $X.\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal O_X$, and the inner tensor product is the pointwise one supplied by Mathlib.
\end{definition}
